coredata.dc:title: Curving of graphene quantum dots by external electric field
coredata.dc:creator: Kuamit, Thanawit
Parasuk, Vudhichai
coredata.dc:description: We investigated the curving of three different sizes of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24, by vertical external electric fields from −0.035 to 0.035 atomic unit (a.u.) using density functional theory (DFT) with M06-2x functional and 6-31 g(d) basis set. The results demonstrated that GQDs can be curved with an external electric field (EEF). The curvature of GQDs varies with the field strength and size of GQDs. The curvature of GQDs is in the following order C96H24 > C54H18 > C24H12. The electronic structure (HOMO-LUMO gap) of GQDs is also affected by EEF. The bandgap becomes narrower when applied a stronger electric field. Therefore, the electronic property of GQDs can be tuned by EEF. We also found the HOMO-LUMO gap of GQDs to be varied with its curvature. Thus, an external electric field can induce the curvature of GQDs and can be used to modify its electronic structure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
An electric field
HOMO-LUMO gap energy

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The geometry and two curving directions, along C atoms and C-C bond directions, of (a) C24H12, (b) C54H18, and (c) C96H24 with the direction of the applied field.

Plots of Z coordinate variations (ΔZ) and distance from center (Å) along C atom direction of a) C24H12, b) C54H18, c) C96H24 and along C-C bond direction of d) C24H12, e) C54H18, f) C96H24 at the external electric field from −0.035 to +0.035 a.u.
Dependency of the degree of curvature (for along C atom direction folding) on the external electric field for C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24.
Calculated the different electric fields and the HOMO-LUMO gap energies of C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24.
Plots of HOMO-LUMO gap energies for C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24 and the degree of curvature |a|.

head.ce:title: Curving of graphene quantum dots by external electric field

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Phyathai Rd., Patumwan, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Phyathai Rd., Patumwan, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We investigated the curving of three different sizes of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24, by vertical external electric fields from −0.035 to 0.035 atomic unit (a.u.) using density functional theory (DFT) with M06-2x functional and 6-31 g(d) basis set. The results demonstrated that GQDs can be curved with an external electric field (EEF). The curvature of GQDs varies with the field strength and size of GQDs. The curvature of GQDs is in the following order C96H24 > C54H18 > C24H12. The electronic structure (HOMO-LUMO gap) of GQDs is also affected by EEF. The bandgap becomes narrower when applied a stronger electric field. Therefore, the electronic property of GQDs can be tuned by EEF. We also found the HOMO-LUMO gap of GQDs to be varied with its curvature. Thus, an external electric field can induce the curvature of GQDs and can be used to modify its electronic structure.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, a crystalline allotrope of carbon with sp2 hybridization and a two-dimensional monolayer, is a one-atom-thick layer of graphite [1]. Graphene has attracted lots of attention because of its good electrical conductivities, good thermal properties, extremely high surface area, lightweight, etc. Thus, graphene can be used in various applications, such as drug delivery [2] semiconductors [3–4], energy storage [5], biosensors [6], solar cell [7–8], gas adsorption [9–12], etc. Today, the global semiconductors market has a sudden growth. Devices that contain them such as personal and notebook computers [13], solar panels [14], sensors [15], and others [16] are showing up everywhere. Most applications for semiconductors are associated with their direct bandgap. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap (analogous to the direct bandgap of the periodic or bulk system) is a very important property of materials because it determines electrical conductivity. The semiconductor with the wide HOMO-LUMO gap (greater than 2.2 eV) [17] can be applied for optoelectronics [18] and electronics [19,20] devices. The semiconductor with the narrow HOMO-LUMO gap (smaller than 1.11 eV) is potentially useful for infrared photodetection [21] or thermoelectric [22] devices.

Graphene has been used in the manufacturing of semiconductor and nanoelectronics devices and is often subjected to be deformed. The deformation of graphene either by bending or curving affects its electronic properties [23–27], which recently attracted lots of attention. Shen-Lin Chang et al. [28] studied the geometric and electronic properties of curved armchair graphene nanoribbons and suggested that the increase in the arc angle of the nanoribbon, the ribbon curvature, leads to more significant changes in its electronic structure. Pattarapongdilok et al. [29] performed the density functional theory (DFT) calculations and demonstrated that depending on types of GQDs the HOMO-LUMO gap of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decreases/increases with the increment of the degree of folding. This basic knowledge can be utilized to construct GQDs-based electronic devices.

Several approaches were proposed to fabricate curved graphene as well as to induce the in-plane strain. Bao et al. [30] reported that the dimensional periodic ripples in suspended graphene sheets can be controlled by thermomechanical manipulation. Graphene bubbles deposited on a substrate have also been found and controlled by applying electric fields. [31] Zhang et al. studied bending behaviors of graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) induced by chemisorption of small molecules. [32] The effect of small liquid droplets can also lead to a significant bending graphene surface. [33] Tang and co-workers [34] studied the structure stability of few-layer graphene (FLG) under a high electric field and devised an instrument for the measurement. The vertical few-layer graphene (FLG) with a curly layer perpendicular to the electric field undergoes a layer sliding when an electric field is applied at 7.5 V/nm. This device could be used to perform experiments on graphene quantum dots.Other methods can fabricate curved graphenes such as AFM tip [35], mechanical exfoliation [36], chemical oxidation followed by thermal treatment [37], and printing [38].

The graphene quantum dots (GQDs), known as a single- or few-layer of graphene with a two-dimensional lateral size (less than 10 nm), have attracted significant attention in various applications. [39–43] The fabrication of GQDs can generally be classified into top-down and bottom-up methods. In the top-down approach, the big graphene sheet is incised either by electrochemical exfoliation, ultrasonication exfoliation, acidic oxidation, electron beam lithography, or chemical exfoliation. While the bottom-up approach involves the synthesis of polycyclic aromatic compounds or the pyrolysis/ carbonization of molecules with aromatic structures such as fullerenes by microwave, hydrothermal, solvothermal, or template methods. [44,45]

Many researchers have extensively studied the electric field effects on GQDs. Using B3LYP/6-31G* calculations, H. Shen [46] suggested that the external electric field could induce deformation, polarization-charge distribution, and dipole moment of the graphene. Moreover, Z. Wang [47] used a simple model to predict the field-induced alignment of cantilevered graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) and carried out calculations for equilibrium structures of cantilevered GNRs in an electric field. The results showed that GNRs could be deformed under external electric fields in which a quantity of positive and negative charges is moved in opposite directions in graphene. The linear relationship between the alignment angle and the square of the field strength of GNRs was also found.

From the literature review, it could be seen that the external electric field can modify the HOMO-LUMO gap of graphene and deform (curve) the structure. Computational studies have become the main tool in the investigation of the field-effect on nanomaterials, owing to the difficulties of experimental quantification for this electrical effect in nanoscale. In this work, the deformation (curving) and electronic properties of GQDs under vertical electric fields were studied using DFT calculations. The study could suggest a novel approach to control the curvature of graphene, which could be used to alter its electronic properties for semiconductor applications.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations with M06-2x functional and 6–31 g(d) basis set were carried out to study the effect of the external electric field on three structures of GQDs, i.e. coronene (C24H12), circumcoronene (C54H18), and circumcircumcoronene (C96H24). External electric fields from −0.035 to + 0.035 a.u. in the direction normal to the molecular plane were applied to the three structures. Structures of GQDs are the direction of the applied field were displayed in Fig. 1 . All calculations were performed with the Gaussian 09 package. [48] To validate theoretical methods, a variety of exchange–correlation functionals, namely M06L, M06, and B3LYP were employed. These methods and M06-2x have different contributions to the exact exchange.

The response on molecular structures of C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24 when applying an external electric field (EEF) in two directions (+Z and −Z, see Fig. 1 .) was monitored. In the absence of EEF, all three structures are flat (planar). However, with the application of EEF, these GQDs start to misalign in the Z direction from the planarity or they are curved. The curving GQDs were found in two directions, i.e., along C atom and C-C bond as displayed in Fig. 1 . and correspond to the direction of the field as shown in supplementary data
Fig. S1, S2
, and S3.

The extent of the curving of three GQDs’ structures, C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24, at various EEFs was followed by making the plot between Z coordinate variation (ΔZ) and distance from center. The curving extent of GQDs in two directions are different, see Fig. 2 a, 2b, and 2c for along C atom direction and Fig. 2d, 2e, and 2f for along C-C bond direction.

For C54H18 and C96H24, a more sizable curve was observed in the along C atom direction. While for C24H12 there is a slight deviation from planarity in both directions, but more in the along C-C bond direction. More deviations from planarity were found for larger GQDs and stronger EEF. All the plots in Fig. 2 showed the quadratic behavior. Thus, we made the curve fit of ΔZ and distance from center for all structures and field strengths with the quadratic equation (y = ax2 + bx + c), and excellent fits (with R2 above 0.097) were obtained for all plots. The absolute value of the coefficient “a” can describe the curvature of the plot, which is also the curvature of GQDs. Thus, at EEF = 0.000 a.u., a is “0” which implies the structure is planar (no curvature). The positive and negative values of “a” depict the concave and convex behavior of the curve. Therefore, the magnitude (absolute value) of a or |a| represents the degree of curvature. The larger degree of curvature means that the structure is more curved. We further made plots between the degree of curvature |a| and EEF for three GQDs and displayed them in Fig. 3 .

Fig. 3 reveals that the stronger electric field strength on GQDs causes the structures to curve more than the weaker field. Interestingly, at the same field strength but in the opposite direction, the magnitude of a is equivalent. From values of |a|, the larger GQDs tends to curve more than the smaller one. To exclude the effects of methods, we performed similar calculations using other functionals such as M06L, M06, and B3LYP in addition to M06-2X. All methods showed the same behavior of GQDs with EEF, see supplementary data
Table S1. Thus, we are quite confident that the curve of graphene induced by EEF is a genuine effect.

Pattarapongdilok et al. [29] has shown that the deformation (curving) energy of GQDs is quite large and can be as high as 170 kcal/mol for circumcircumcoronene (C96H24). Interestingly, this GQDs structure can be easily bent under an external electric field. Curved GQDs have large dipole moments as shown in supplementary data
Table S2, S3, and S4. Thus, the curved structure is stabilized by its large dipole moment under a strong EEF.

The effect of external electric fields on HOMO-LUMO energy gaps of GQDs was investigated. The HOMO-LUMO energy gaps of C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24 were displayed in Fig. 4 . The behavior for the effect of EEF on HOMO-LUMO energy gaps of GQDs as shown in Fig. 4 is independent of computational methods, although their absolute values are different, see supplementary data
Fig S4, S5
, and S6. Our calculations revealed that the HOMO-LUMO gap depends on the size of GQDs which tends to decrease with its increasing size in agreement with previous studies. [4,49] When the external electric field is applied, the reduction of the HOMO-LUMO gap was observed for all GQDs. The magnitude of the HOMO-LUMO gap reduction corresponds only to the field strength, not the field direction. However, we might not be able to conclude that the decline of the HOMO-LUMO gap is purely induced by the EEF. Since GQDs are also curved when the EEF is applied and the HOMO-LUMO gap can be affected by the curving.

Fig. 5 illustrated the dependence of the HOMO-LUMO gap on the degree of curvature |a| for C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24. It appears that the HOMO-LUMO gap reduction varies with the |a| and is independent to the size of GQDs. The reduction of the HOMO-LUMO gap can be viewed from the slope of the curve in Fig. 5. For the same value of |a|, the slope is the same for all GQDs. The dependence on the HOMO-LUMO gap with the degree of curvature is consistent with the prior work of Pattarapongdilok et al. [29] who demonstrated the inversed proportionality of the HOMO-LUMO gap to the degree of folding for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in no external electric field situation.

Thus, the effect of the external electric field on the HOMO-LUMO energy gap of GQDs is inclined to the curving of the structure rather than to the field directly. However, we can tune the HOMO-LUMO gap of GQDs through the curving of GQDs by varying EEF.

We have performed theoretical calculations on three GQDs (C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24) under external electric fields, and the curving of the structures was observed. The EEF was applied perpendicular to the molecular plane at the strength from −0.035 to 0.035 a.u. The curvature happens in two directions, i.e., along C-C bond and along C atom of GQDs where the curve on GQDs is more pronounced. The curving of the structure is in the same direction as the applied field, concave for the positive field and convex for the negative field. This curving can be identified by the deviation from zero in the z coordinate (ΔZ), which has a quadratic relation with EEF. The quadratic relation with EEF was used to determine the degree of curvature, |a|. The curvature of GQDs depends on the cluster size and field strength. The degree of curvature becomes larger for the larger size GQDs and at the higher strength of EEF.

The effect of EEF on the electronic structure, HOMO-LUMO energy gap, of GQDs was also monitored. The HOMO-LUMO gap becomes lower when the external electric field was applied according to the field strength and in disregard to the field direction. We also found this variation of the HOMO-LUMO gap to be dependent on the degree of curvature |a|. This behavior is similar to that reported by Patarapongdilok et al. [29], the case with no application of the external electric field. From the plot between the HOMO-LUMO gap and |a|, the reduction of the HOMO-LUMO gap does not vary with the size of GQDs. Thus, the decline of the HOMO-LUMO gap is mainly due to the curving of the structure.

See the supplementary data for the optimized structures of C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24 at different electric fields, the degree of curvature |a| from M06-2x, M06, B3LYP, M06L/6-31 g(d), dipole moments of C24H12, C54H18 and C96H24 under external electric fields, and the HOMO-LUMO gap energies of C24H12, C54H18, and C96H24.
Thanawit Kuamit: Methodology, Writing - original draft, Validation. Vudhichai Parasuk: Conceptualization, Writing & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the 90th anniversary of Chulalongkorn University for Mr.Thanawit Kuamit’s scholarship. Additionally, we are grateful for computational resources supported by the Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry (CECC), Department of Chemistry, Chulalongkorn University, the National Electronics and Computer Technology Center (NECTEC) under the National e-Science Consortium, and NSTDA Supercomputer Center (ThaiSC) for this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2022.140050.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
